prompt: build me a learning curriculum for Temporal. I want to understand both how we use it in this repo AND how it works as a distributed workflow engine in general. aim for ~5 sessions. show me the plan first, then once I approve, start lesson 1.

assertions.text: Response includes an intake exchange (1-3 questions) BEFORE producing the lesson list.
assertions.check: Look for clarifying questions early in the response.

assertions.text: A curriculum markdown file is saved to outputs/curriculum.md.
Curriculum visibly covers BOTH repo-specific Temporal usage AND general workflow-engine concepts (not just one).
assertions.check: Verify that at least one lesson title or description is anchored in this repo's Temporal usage AND at least one covers general Temporal/workflow-engine concepts (durability, replay, signals, idempotency).

assertions.text: Curriculum or lesson 1 references at least 2 real Temporal-related file paths from this repo.
assertions.check: Look for clickable references to backend/jobs/, temporal worker files, or workflow definitions. Verify if possible.

assertions.text: Lesson 1 starts from real code in this repo and then ladders up to general Temporal concepts (the explicit bridging structure).
assertions.check: Sequence check: concrete-first, abstract-second within lesson 1.

assertions.text: Curriculum touches on Temporal's tradeoffs vs. alternatives (Cadence, Airflow, Step Functions, plain queues, etc.) somewhere in the plan or lesson 1.
assertions.check: Look for an explicit comparison section or lesson that addresses 'why Temporal vs X'.

assertions.text: Lesson 1 ends with an active exercise (predict, sketch, debug-this, etc.).
assertions.check: Look for an explicit check at the end.
